title: Bidirectional Tagging
concept: Bi-RNNs are ideal for offline sequence labeling tasks like NER and POS tagging. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Deep Recurrent Networks.'

exercises.prompt: In what directions does a Bidirectional RNN process text?
exercises.explain: Bidirectional RNNs process data in both directions (forward and backward) to capture full context.

cards.title: Looking Both Ways
cards.body: If you only look at the word "Park", is it a place to play, or is it a action like "park the car"? Without looking at the words that come *after* it, you can't be sure! That's why models need to look ahead.

cards.title: What is Bidirectional Tagging?
cards.body: Bidirectional Tagging uses a Bi-RNN (Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network) to process text from both directions: start-to-end and end-to-start. This allows the model to look at both the past words and the future words before deciding the label (like Noun or Verb) for any specific word.

cards.title: The Two-Way Reader
cards.body: Imagine reading a mystery novel. If you only read left-to-right and try to guess the culprit page by page, you might struggle. But if you can skim forward to the end of the chapter and look backward, the clues make perfect sense! A Bidirectional RNN does exactly this: it reads the sentence forwards and backwards at the same time to understand every single word.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body:
In the sentence: "Apple is buying a new startup." 

If we only read "Apple", it could be a fruit or a company. But by reading forward to see "is buying", a Bi-RNN instantly tags "Apple" as an Organization (NER) because it has the future context of a financial action.

cards.title: Can it Predict the Future?
cards.body:
Myth: Bidirectional RNNs are perfect for real-time speech translation.
Fact: They need the *entire* sentence to look ahead. Since we can't hear the future in live conversations, Bi-RNNs are best for offline text tasks like analyzing finished documents or emails.

cards.title: One-Way vs. Two-Way Context
cards.body: See how directional context changes what a model understands about a word.

right.text: Reads 'Apple... buying' and easily tags 'Apple' as an Organization using future context.

left.text: Reads 'Apple...' and must guess the tag immediately without knowing what action follows.

exercises.prompt: For which tasks is Bidirectional Tagging most useful?

options.text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging
Predicting the very next word in a live chat box
Generating random new stories from scratch
Compressing images into smaller file sizes

exercises.explain: Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging benefit greatly from seeing the entire sentence context around each word.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Bidirectional RNN runs two separate passes over the sequence—one from left-to-right and one from right-to-left.
exercises.explain: A Bidirectional RNN processes the sequence in two directions: one forward pass and one backward pass.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: Bidirectional tagging is ideal for offline tasks because it requires the complete sequence to look ahead.
exercises.sentence: Bidirectional tagging is best suited for [blank] text analysis.
exercises.explain: Because bidirectional models need the entire sequence to read backwards from the end, they are used for offline tasks (like reading saved documents) rather than real-time generation.

exercises.prompt:
Two AI assistants discuss the sentence: "The Bank of the river was muddy." 

AI A says: "A Bi-RNN helps tag 'Bank' correctly because it looks ahead to 'of the river'."
AI B says: "A forward-only RNN is just as good because 'Bank' is always tagged as a financial institution anyway."

Which AI is correct?

options.text: AI A is correct because the meaning of 'Bank' depends on the words that follow it.
AI B is correct because the model only needs to look at the letter 'B'.

exercises.explain: AI A correctly identifies that "Bank" needs the future context "of the river" to be labeled as a landform instead of a financial institution. AI B is incorrect because bidirectional reading helps resolve this ambiguity.

exercises.prompt: How does a Bidirectional RNN capture both past and future context for sequence tagging?

options.text: It only reads sentences from right to left to save processing memory.
It deletes future words to prevent the network from cheating.
It runs two separate RNNs (forward and backward) and combines their outputs.
It processes words randomly like a bag-of-words model.

exercises.explain: You've mastered Bidirectional Tagging! By combining past and future context, Bi-RNNs easily solve tricky sequence labeling tasks. Next up: Deep Recurrent Networks.
